title: Pasta e fagioli
subtitle: Thick Italian pasta and borlotti bean soup.
excerpt: Borlotti beans simmered with a soffritto base, then cooked with small pasta until the soup is thick and the pasta is barely al dente. A full meal in a single bowl. One of the great preparations of the Italian south.
sourceNotes: Pasta e fagioli (pasta and beans) is one of the defining cucina povera preparations of southern Italy and the Veneto, though versions of it exist across the entire peninsula. Historically it was a peasant staple that combined two of the most available protein sources: dried beans and pasta. The consistency varies by region and cook: Neapolitan versions are almost soupy, Venetian versions are thicker and more risotto-like. The version here aims for a thick, cohesive consistency where the pasta and beans are inseparable.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the bean base ahead and add pasta only when reheating, as the pasta absorbs the liquid as it sits.

content.text: The consistency of pasta e fagioli is somewhere between soup and stew. The pasta cooks in the soup itself rather than in separate salted water, which means it absorbs the starchy bean liquid and becomes part of the dish rather than floating in it. Cook the pasta to just under al dente: it continues cooking as the dish rests.

Mashing some of the beans against the side of the pot is the traditional thickening method. Rosemary is the classic herb here; the combination of rosemary and beans is one of the characteristic flavour pairs of Tuscan and central Italian cooking.

items.prepNote: small pasta: ditalini, tubetti, or broken spaghetti

content.text: Build the base and add beans

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Cook the onion, carrot, and celery for 8–10 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and rosemary and cook for 1 minute. Add the tinned tomatoes and cook for 3 minutes. Add the drained {{borlotti-beans}} of borlotti beans and {{stock-vegetable}} of stock. Bring to a simmer. Mash roughly a third of the beans against the side of the pot. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Cook the pasta in the soup

Remove the rosemary sprigs. Add {{pasta-dried}} of pasta directly to the simmering soup. Cook for 2 minutes less than the packet time, stirring frequently to prevent sticking. Remove from the heat and rest for 5 minutes — the pasta will continue cooking. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Finish with a drizzle of olive oil.

items.symptom: Pasta has absorbed all the liquid and the dish is too thick
items.cause: Pasta cooked too long, or left sitting in the residual heat.
items.fix: Add a splash of hot water and stir through. This is common as the dish sits.

items.symptom: Soup is watery with the pasta not binding into it
items.cause: Beans not mashed enough, or too much stock.
items.fix: Mash more beans against the side of the pot. The starch from the beans is what thickens the soup.

items.cause: Cooked for too long in the soup, which is hotter than salted water.
items.fix: Cook for at least 2 minutes less than the packet time and check earlier. The pasta continues cooking in the residual heat.

items.cause: Under-salted, or the soffritto was not cooked long enough.
items.fix: Taste and add more salt. Bean dishes need generous seasoning. Cook the base for a full 10 minutes.

content.text: Pasta e fagioli has fed Italians across every social class for centuries, adjusting only in the quality of the oil and the cut of the pasta. In its grandest form it uses fresh borlotti beans from the pod and handmade pasta; in its most everyday form it uses tins of beans and dried pasta from the back of the cupboard. The dish is the same. It represents one of the most useful properties of cucina povera cooking: that the structure of a dish can remain fixed while the quality of ingredients scales from peasant to restaurant.
